The Future of Biology
Is Agentic

From orchestration to validation: agent-native skill libraries as the path to clinical-grade genomic AI
Manuel Corpas
Senior Lecturer in Genomics, AI, and Data Science
University of Westminster
UK DRI seminar · Imperial College London · 29 April 2026
Two papers underpin this talk. First, a Perspective in bioRxiv with Segun Fatumo and Heinner Guio defining what agentic genomics is, what it is not, and the validation framework we propose. Second, an empirical benchmark with Alfredo Iacoangeli, Fatumo, and Guio, in submission to Briefings in Bioinformatics, that tests whether the framework actually delivers on clinical-grade pharmacogenomics. TIMING: 1 min.
The shift

Two Waves of LLMs in Biology

First wave: information retrieval

  • Summarising papers, answering pathway questions, extracting structured data from text.
  • Useful but incremental.

Second wave: autonomous execution

  • Modern LLMs can write, debug, and execute code.
  • Connected to file systems, databases, command-line tools, they plan multi-step operations and adapt on intermediate results.
  • The researcher's role shifts from producing analyses to evaluating them.

Corpas, Fatumo, Guio. Agentic Genomics: From Pipeline Automation to Autonomous Validation. bioRxiv 2026.

The point I want to plant: this is not a chatbot story. The interesting thing is autonomous tool use with consequence. Once the model is acting, the rate-limiting step shifts. TIMING: 1.5 min.
Definition

Defining Agentic Genomics

"The use of autonomous AI agents, powered by large language models and operating within domain-constrained skill libraries, to discover, plan, execute, and iteratively refine multi-step genomic analyses, where the agent exercises runtime decision-making over tool selection, parameterisation, error handling, and output evaluation."

1. Autonomy

Decisions during execution, not a static workflow specification.

2. Domain constraint

Operates within a structured library of validated skills, not ad hoc code.

3. Iterative refinement

Evaluates intermediate results, modifies its approach, recovers from errors.

Plus a fourth: natural-language mediation. The four conditions are jointly necessary. Empirically falsifiable via the perturbation test: a system fails to qualify as agentic if perturbed intermediate outputs do not alter its execution strategy.

These four conditions are deliberately strict. They exclude workflow automation (Nextflow, Snakemake, Galaxy, no runtime decisions). They exclude AutoML (search within a fixed space). They exclude LLM-assisted scripting (you execute, not the agent). And they exclude general-purpose biomedical copilots (information retrieval, no multi-step execution against real data). TIMING: 2 min.
Figure 1 · bioRxiv 2026

The Paradigm Shift: Code Production to Validation

(A) Traditional workflow. Researcher writes code, configures tools, runs pipelines, interprets results. The bottleneck is code production.   (B) Agentic workflow. Researcher describes intent in natural language; an AI agent discovers and executes skills from a modular library; researcher validates results. The bottleneck shifts to validation and judgement.

This is the central diagram of the bioRxiv Perspective. The skills shown in panel B are real ClawBio skills: pharmacogenomics, variant annotation, ancestry estimation, drug safety, genome QC, PRS calculation, nutri-genomics, structural variants. Same audience question for the rest of the talk: what does it take to make panel B trustworthy? TIMING: 1.5 min.
The new bottleneck

The Validation Bottleneck: Silent, Plausible-Looking Failure

  • AI agents produce results faster than humans can verify them. New class of risk: high-throughput, low-validation science.
  • Documented failure modes across independent systems:
    • AutoBA: pipelines omitting critical steps, wrong tool for data type.
    • Single-cell agents: incomplete experimental designs, inconsistent recommendations for identical queries.
    • Boiko et al. (Nature 2023): syntactically correct, scientifically invalid protocols passing basic execution checks.
    • ClawBio early audit: a skill silently returned "all normal" results for 51 drugs when given an empty input file.
  • The pattern: silent degradation to plausible-looking but incorrect results.
Each of these is from an independently developed system. The convergence is the point: this is structural, not a one-off bug. The 51-drug ClawBio incident is mine, surfaced by a community auditor. We discovered it because the platform is open. That's an argument for transparency. TIMING: 2 min.
Framework

A Tiered Validation Framework

Research-grade

Hypothesis exploration, exploratory analysis. Unit tests, adversarial inputs, minimal logging, all outputs reviewed. False positives tolerable if caught.

Benchmarked

Publishable analyses, method comparison. Validated on public references (GIAB for variant calling, curated scRNA-seq benchmarks). Independent benchmarking, published metrics, documented failure modes.

Clinical-grade

Patient care, diagnostic reporting, pharmacogenomics. External multi-site validation, FDA/EMA alignment, signed reproducibility bundles, BioCompute Objects, version-locked dependencies. CLIA/CAP compliance.

Validation proportional to consequence. Agent platforms must enforce tier boundaries and expose deterministic replay: a logged sequence of decisions must be exactly reproducible.

Corpas, Fatumo & Guio. bioRxiv 2026 · Table 2.

This is the structural response to silent failure. The tiers are not prescriptive labels; they are calibrated to risk. A skill at research-grade can be invoked freely for hypothesis generation. The same skill cannot be invoked for clinical use without external multi-site validation, signed bundles, and deterministic replay. The next slides ask whether any currently shippable skill can satisfy clinical-grade. TIMING: 2 min.
Empirical question

Can a Plain-Text SKILL.md Reach Clinical-Grade?

  • Domain: pharmacogenomics. Genotype to phenotype to drug recommendation, ground truth from CPIC guidelines.
  • Stakes: real. DPYD rs3918290 T/T + standard fluorouracil = potentially lethal.
  • If specification cannot improve reliability here, where the guideline is fixed and the consequences are clinically measurable, it is unlikely to help in less structured domains.

Models: Claude Opus 4, Sonnet 4; GPT-5.2, GPT-4.1, o3, o4-mini; Gemini 2.5 Flash; DeepSeek V3; Mistral Large 2. Two conditions (with vs without ClawBio specification), three independent runs.

Population contexts: European (Corpasome family WGS), admixed Latin American (Peruvian Genome Project, 109 WGS, 7 sub-populations), East African (Uganda Genome Resource, 6,407 WGS). Curated by Heinner Guio and Segun Fatumo respectively. TIMING: 2 min.
Result 1 · without specification

Frontier LLMs Are Not Yet Clinical-Grade

92.4%
Mean phenotype
accuracy (A1)
61%
Worst model
(Gemini 2.5 Flash)
7
DPYD lethal-case
errors (of 67 parsed)
87.6%
Perfect 3-run
consistency
  • Same model, same input, different runs: stochastic drift across reruns.
  • "92% accuracy" sounds fine. For the DPYD homozygote, 92% means roughly 1 in 12 patients receives a potentially lethal recommendation.
  • Failure modes: confabulated population data, format non-compliance, miscalled metaboliser status.

Tier A clinical correctness across 9 models. Phenotype accuracy, drug recommendation, clinical safety. Grey = without specification shows wide spread and long error bars, especially for Gemini 2.5 Flash and o3 on drug recommendation. Green = with ClawBio specification previewed for context; the with-spec mechanism is the focus of the next slide.

Average accuracy hides the tail. The tail is what gets people hurt. The consistency rate (87.6 percent) is computed as the fraction of model x test-case x population combinations where all parsed runs agreed and were correct, with at least 2 of 3 runs producing parseable output. Source data and code are public. TIMING: 2 min.
Result 2 · with ClawBio specification

Specification Eliminates Stochastic Variation

Fraction of correct phenotype calls per model (rows) and test case (columns), aggregated across populations and runs. Left: without specification, scattered errors with red cells (worst case 0%). Right: with ClawBio SKILL.md specification, 100% across all 290 evaluable model x test-case x population combinations, on all three runs.

Visually striking, scientifically the central result. Three failure modes eliminated in a single intervention: stochastic variation, format non-compliance, miscalls. Tier B (contextual narrative) drops, which is the trade we want for clinical grade. TIMING: 2 min.
Result 2b · multidimensional view

Specification Lifts All Six Evaluation Dimensions

Per-model performance across six dimensions: phenotype accuracy, drug recommendation, clinical safety, reasoning chain, domain grounding, dataset specificity. Left: without specification, models occupy uneven, partial polygons; reasoning chain and dataset specificity are the worst-performing axes. Right: with ClawBio specification, models converge to near-maximal coverage on every axis, with one residual exception (Mistral Large 2 on reasoning chain).

Consistency (slide 9) is binary: did the runs agree and were they correct. The radar shows what specification fixes beneath that headline: reasoning-chain quality, dataset grounding, and domain-specific reasoning all rise. The exception (Mistral on reasoning chain) is a useful counter-example for the Q&A: spec is necessary, not sufficient. TIMING: 1.5 min.
Result 3 · equity

The Population Gap: 6 of 7 Lethal Errors Were Non-European

Phenotype accuracy and perfect-consistency rate by population context (without specification). DPYD lethal case: 1 EUR error, 3 AMR errors, 3 AFR errors. With specification, accuracy is 100% across all populations; the equity gap closes entirely.

This is not a curiosity. It is the structural argument for why the specification layer matters: models default to European resources because that is what is statistically abundant in training data. The skill carries curated population-specific data from Fatumo (East African) and Guio (Latin American) that the model would otherwise confabulate. TIMING: 2 min.
Implication

Equity Must Be Engineered, Not Declared

  • Equity-aware default configurations. Skills query population-stratified frequency databases (gnomAD stratifications, AfriCArGene) instead of aggregate European-dominated frequencies.
  • Population-aware model selection. Skill metadata declares which populations the underlying models were validated against; agents flag or refuse out-of-scope use.
  • Bias detection at execution. Population-mismatch signals (anomalous PCA projections, calibration failures) as automated QC, analogous to standard sequencing QC.
  • Measurable equity metrics. Skill registries report (i) population composition of validation sets, (ii) ancestry-stratified performance, (iii) proportion of skills validated on non-European populations.

A skill that performs well on European-ancestry data but has not been evaluated on African or Latin American populations should not be considered fully validated, regardless of its technical performance on the populations it has been tested against.

Equity becomes a first-class validation criterion alongside accuracy and reproducibility. The benchmark's population dimension is a worked example of this. We argue this is structural: the absence of equity-aware design should be treated with the same seriousness as the absence of input validation. TIMING: 2 min.
Closing thesis

Five Principles for Responsible Agentic Genomics

1. Domain expertise is irreducible

Agents amplify capacity; they do not replace judgement.

2. Validation proportional to consequence

Tiered framework, enforced at platform level.

3. Transparency is non-negotiable

Failures reported openly and fixed publicly.

4. Skills must be testable by design

Test suites for normal, edge, and adversarial inputs. Untested = unvalidated.

5. Equity must be engineered

Population-aware defaults, bias detection, equity metrics in skill registries.

The central thesis

Agentic genomics shifts the bottleneck from pipeline construction to validation. The empirical benchmark shows that a plain-text skill specification can satisfy two of the three clinical-grade requirements (traceability, cross-population invariance). External multi-site validation is the open work.

Source papers: Corpas, Fatumo, Guio. bioRxiv 2026 · Corpas, Iacoangeli, Fatumo, Guio. Briefings in Bioinformatics, in submission 2026.

Close on the central thesis, not on a pitch. Read the principles, then the closing line. The question is no longer whether agentic genomics will be adopted; it is whether the field will establish the standards required to make it trustworthy before it becomes ubiquitous. TIMING: 2 min, leaving 5 to 7 min Q&A within the 30-min recorded slot.
